title: When The Bell Blew Up
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That's the boiler at The Bell, mates! Tumble out, Ned, neck and crop, 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Never mind your hat and coat, man, weן¿½ll be wanted on the job.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Barneyן¿½s driving, Harveyן¿½s stoking, God help all the hands on top!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bring along the brandy, some one. Donן¿½t stand like an image, Bob;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Grab those shirts, theyן¿½ll all be needed. Rugs and candles, thatן¿½s all right.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bet your lives, boys, weן¿½ll have lots of doctorן¿½s work to do to-night!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Didnן¿½t she thunder? Scot! I thought the universe had gone to smash.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Take the track through Peetreeן¿½s paddock, make the smartest time you know.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Barney swore her plates were rotten, but poor Bill was always rash.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And his missus, heaven help her!, they were spliced a month ago.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Down the track we raced together, up the hill, then oן¿½er the claim
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Saw the steam-clouds hanging thickly, lustrous with the glow of flame.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Boiler-house in hopeless ruins, engines wrecked and smoke-stack gone;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bricks and shingles widely scattered, and the shattered boiler bare.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Five men missed!ן¿½ ן¿½Buck in, you fellows; get your freest action on;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Keep the fire back from the timbers, God knows who is under there.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sprag that knocker. How it rattles! Bracemanן¿½s nowhere, Colemanן¿½s Joe.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tell them what has happened, Ryan. They will have to wait below.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As we fought the fires, the women, pale and tearful gathered round.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That you, Peter? Thanks to Heaven!ן¿½ ן¿½Thereן¿½s my Harry! God is good!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Praise the Lord, theyן¿½ve got our lad safe! Joe the braceman has been found!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Down between the tips they found him, pinned there by a log of wood.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Battery boys are safe. Mack saw them hiding under Peetreeן¿½s ricks.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They just up and cut from under when it started raining bricks.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Only two now, Bill and Barney. Still we laboured might and main
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Mid the ruins round the boiler where the shattered walls were stacked.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then his wife discovered Barney, dazed and black, but right as rain;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said he didnן¿½t know what hit him, ן¿½thought the crack of doom had cracked;ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He had landed on the sand-heap, thirty yards or so away.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½God is mighty good to sinners,ן¿½ murmured Geordie. ן¿½Let us pray.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fifty voices called on Harvey, and we worked like horses all,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Delving down amongst the timber, burnt and knocked about, but gay.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Lend a hand, here, every man; heן¿½s pinned beneath the outer wall!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All together. Now youן¿½ve got him. Gently does it. Thatן¿½s O.K.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Scalded! Yes, and right arm broken. Pass some brandy, one of you.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cheer, ye devils! Give it lip, lads. Heן¿½s alive and kicking, too!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Give him air, now. Make a track there. Let him see his missus first.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Whereן¿½s his wife?ן¿½ The women wondered. She had not been seen all night.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Someone whispered she was timid, that she dared not face the worst.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Harvey smiled despite his troubles. ן¿½Boys, sheן¿½s fainted, sheן¿½s all right.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So we bore him gaily home, and as he saw the gateway near
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bill tried hard to lead the chorus when we gave a rousing cheer.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Stop, for Godן¿½s sake!ן¿½ In the garden, where her life blood tinged the vine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Prone poor Harveyן¿½s wife was lying, in the moon- light, cold and gray.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There the flying bolt had struck her as she ran towards the mine.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We could guess the truth too well, and near a broken firebar lay.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Carrol, kneeling down beside her, gently raised the wounded head,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And we bent to catch his whisper, and he answered sadly, ן¿½Dead!ן¿½
meaning:
About: Edward George Dyson (1865-1931) was an Australian poet, journalist and short story writer.

He was born at Morrisons near Ballarat in March 1865. His father, George Dyson, arrived in Australia in 1852 and after working on various diggings became a mining engineer, his mother came from a life of refinement in England. The family led a roving life during Dyson's childhood, moving successively to Alfredton, Bendigo, Ballarat and Alfredton again. Unconsciously the boy was storing for future use the life of the miners, farmers and bushmen, among whom he lived. At 12 he began to work as an assistant to a travelling draper, after that was a whimboy in a mine, and for two or three years an assistant in a factory at Melbourne. This was followed by work in a newspaper office. At 19 he began writing verse, and a few years later embarked on a life of free-lance journalism which lasted until his death. His first notable work was "The Golden Shanty", which appeared in the Bulletin, and many other short stories followed. In 1896 he published a volume of poems, Rhymes from the Mines, and in 1898 the first collection of his short stories, Below and On Top. In 1901 his first long story The Gold-stealers was published in London, which was followed by In the Roaring Fifties in 1906. In the same year appeared Fact'ry 'Ands, a series of more or less connected sketches dealing with factory life in Melbourne in a vein of humour. Various other stories and collections of stories were published in the Bookstall Series and will be found listed in Miller's Australian Literature. Another volume of verse Hello, Soldier! appeared in 1919. All through the years Dyson did an enormous amount of work until he broke down under the strain and died after a long illness on 22 August 1931. He married Miss Jackson who survived him with one daughter.

Edward Dyson was the brother of Will Dyson and Ambrose Dyson.
